input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 2 and its table: Comparison of experimental spring-back angle (degree) with simulations using constant E and varied E for V-bending. Data: at bend angle 160°, exp. 0.9, const. E 1.0, varied E 1.15; 140°, 1.25, 1.1, 1.3; 120°, 1.25, 1.1, 1.35; 100°, 1.35, 1.15, 1.35; 90°, 1.4, 1.15, 1.35. Conclusion: varied E improves prediction accuracy.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 2 and accompanying table: V-bending spring-back comparison (values in degrees). At bend angle 160°: experimental 0.9°, simulated constant E 1.0°, simulated varied E 1.15°. At 140°: 1.25 / 1.1 / 1.3. At 120°: 1.25 / 1.1 / 1.35. At 100°: 1.35 / 1.15 / 1.35. At 90°: 1.4 / 1.15 / 1.35. Varied elastic modulus simulation predicts spring-back more accurately than constant modulus.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 4a data points extracted: Strain 0.0% stress ~0 MPa for all curves; 0.1% stress ~150 (A), ~200 (B), ~180 (C), ~160 (D), ~170 (E); 0.2% ~200, ~300, ~250, ~220, ~240; 0.3% ~220, ~380, ~360, ~340, ~370 MPa. Fig. 4b: Elastic modulus gradient vs. strain showing rapid decrease from initial ~250 GPa to ~0 GPa at 0.30% strain for some conditions.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 4(b) schematic: Gradient of stress-strain curve (representing elastic modulus) for pre-strained specimen decreases continuously from initial loading through the elastic region, unlike annealed specimen where modulus is nearly constant until yielding.

input_candidates.truth_value: Equation (1): X = (2 D t)^{1/2} used to estimate carbon diffusion distance for heat treatment design (373 K, 90 h).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 12 data table (nano-indentation near grain boundary after CMP, SPCE): Position X [μm] vs Er [GPa] for three lines (Y=0,1,2). Y=0: X=0 ~280, X=5 ~180, X=10 ~120, X=15 ~160, X=20 ~140. Y=1: X=0 ~340, X=5 ~220, X=10 ~200, X=15 ~220, X=20 ~200. Y=2: X=0 ~260, X=5 ~200, X=10 ~220, X=15 ~180, X=20 ~160. Modulus decrease confirmed near X=5–10 μm corresponding to grain boundary.

input_candidates.truth_value: Equation (2): Reduced modulus Er = (√π/(2√A))S and P/A used in Berkovich nano-indentation analysis.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Possible mechanisms for elastic modulus decrease after plastic deformation: increase of residual stress, micro cracks, and variation in dislocation structure. Density reduction due to plastic deformation is less than 1% and deemed insufficient to explain the large modulus drop.

input_candidates.truth_value: Equation (3): For spherical indenter, he = (9 P^2 / (16 R Er^2))^{1/3} based on Hertz contact theory.
input_candidates.prediction_value: In mild steel, dislocations are locked by carbon and other solutes via the Cottrell effect before plastic deformation. During deformation, dislocations are released and the Cottrell atmosphere is not recovered at room temperature because the diffusion rate of C in the Fe matrix is very low.

input_candidates.truth_value: Dislocation mechanism: Movable dislocations released from Cottrell atmospheres during plastic deformation pile up at grain boundaries; during unloading they move back, reducing apparent elastic modulus. Heat treatment at 373 K for 90 h partially recovers Cottrell locking, restoring modulus.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Nano-indentation spherical tip nominal radius R = 50 μm; actual radius was found to be much smaller than nominal, affecting absolute modulus values but not the spatial distribution trend.

candidates.reason: LLM judged same-field values as semantically matched.
